questions.text: When a friend describes a loss, I feel a strong motivation to help them recover.
I find that I can compartmentalize others' emotional situations and continue with my day unaffected.
I believe that the measure of a good person is how seriously they take the suffering of others.
I have found that caring too deeply about others' problems makes it harder to help them effectively.
I find it morally impossible to accept others' unfair treatment without wanting to intervene.
I am comfortable making decisions that cause short-term distress to others when I believe the outcome is better overall.
I often feel compelled to check in on people I know are going through a hard time, even when they have not asked.
I think the most helpful thing you can offer someone in pain is a clear head, not shared tears.
The suffering of strangers in distant places affects me in a way that feels personal and immediate.
I have been described as logical or cool-headed in situations where others are emotionally overwhelmed.
When I see suffering, I feel a moral pull to respond before I even begin to analyze the situation.
I believe that professional effectiveness in caring professions requires the ability to detach from individual cases.
I feel that ignoring someone's pain when I could help is a form of moral failure.
I can listen to emotionally heavy stories and set them down completely when the conversation is over.
When I see others in pain, I feel a moral pull to respond that outweighs my own comfort.
I think that people who become emotionally flooded by others' problems are less equipped to actually solve them.
Witnessing others' suffering makes it impossible for me to remain indifferent.
I value my ability to remain objective when those around me are in emotional distress.
